message.content: Adversaries may exploit Windows accessibility features to establish persistence and elevate privileges by executing malicious content triggered by key combinations before user login. Commonly targeted features include "sticky keys" (activated by pressing Shift five times) and the utility launched with Windows + U. Attackers often replace accessibility binaries or modify registry pointers to these binaries, enabling unauthorized access via the login screen. This technique can provide SYSTEM-level privileges, particularly through Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) sessions. Notable groups like APT29, APT3, APT41, Axiom, Deep Panda, and Fox Kitten have leveraged this method for persistence and privilege escalation. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strict file integrity monitoring, ensuring binaries are digitally signed, and utilizing Windows File or Resource Protection (WFP/WRP) to safeguard critical system files. Regularly auditing and monitoring registry changes and accessibility feature configurations can also help detect and prevent exploitation.
